gap_reviews.text:
So glad I finally made it here to sample the heat of Chili Szechuan. 

My friends from Chengdu are living in New York for several months, and this was their first visit to Philadelphia. After showing them around the shut down historic sites in Old City (Carpenters' Hall was open), they wanted to see the Penn campus. They were hungry, and Chili Szechuan jumped to mind. Why not give them some home cooking?  They would tell me how authentic the cuisine was.

Driving up Baltimore Avenue for the first time since living at 48th and Baltimore back in the day, I was impressed with the changes on the street.  So much positive street life. And, right in the middle of the 4600 block sat Chili Szechuan.  Parking right across the street. We were in luck!

We started with Dumplings with Chili Oil and Dan Dan Noodles with Ground Pork. Both were as good as I have ever had. Spicy, succulent, and about 3 Alarm on the Volcano Scale.  We followed with Shredded Pork with Green Pepper (5 Alarm), Lotus Root with Pickled Garlic (3 Alarm) and BenBen Chicken (4 Alarm).  All varied as to heat, texture, and undertone. The BenBen is served cold and in skin.  All enjoyable.  But, I wanted to know what my friends thought of the dishes. 

They devoured almost everything on the table covered with dishes. They are both small people, so I was amazed how they managed to eat more than I did.  Halfway through the meal they asked the waiter where the chef was from.  When he said, Chengdu, they said they were also from the city. A minute later he exited the kitchen and sat down with us to talk about home.  They switched from Mandarin to Chengduese (a subdialect of Szechuanese), and I was lost.  But, through their animated conversation I knew they were comparing notes about their lives here and in China. My friends clearly loved the food.  They exchanged business cards, and hoped to see each other again next time in Philadelphia. 

As we walked out, and the owner/chef said goodbye again, my friends thanked him for the best meal they had eaten in the USA.  It was like being home in their own kitchen.  I took that to mean that they were very happy with the meal. 

Seeing their enjoyment, and tasting the authenticity of the dishes, Chili Szechuan is as good as it gets for real Szechuan cuisine in this city.  Some might find the small confines of the shop more suitable for take out, and the single unisex bathroom a bit unpleasant, but if you are looking for the real deal in hot, spicy, numbing food, this is your Shangri-La.

10.27.13 - Ok. Here's the skinny on a comparison with Han Dynasty.  There really is none. While both have quality Szechuan style cuisine, the similarities stop there.  Han D is an upscale attractive forum for taking a date to an impressive dinner.  It is pricey and self aware of the experience it is creating.  Chili S could care less about that. It is in business for one reason - to turn out totally authentic hot numbing dishes that leave you in tears, and wondering when your tongue will come back online.  The atmosphere is basic Chengdu heat, as you find on every street and alley in the burgs of Szechuan Province.  It is small and unassuming.  There is only one bathroom.  You can hear the cooks arguing in the kitchen and the woks and pans banging on the stove.  But, if food is what you care about, and you are lucky enough to be with someone who really enjoys unpretentiousness and loves you for your own, then you can save a bundle tonight, and impress her next week with HanD. Both provide a REAL experience on the opposite ends of the lux scale.

It is time to revisit my past reviews of House of Pho, and update my impressions and ranking for this ever improving Vietnamese restaurant.  

Returning for the first time in a few months, the first thing I noticed was the presence of booths along the front windows.  We sat there, and the additional privacy was felt immediately.  Great touch!  They also had moved the drink menu onto a table card for immediate perusal and quick ordering.  Smart move to save the time of waiting for the arrival of the menu.  We selected the Mango Smoothy and Coconut Tuoi, and had them at the table within minutes, and before we ordered the victuals. The Smoothie was sweet and creamy, just as I remembered it, and the Tuoi was a mild coconut infused water with thin Coconut shavings in the glass.  Mildly sweet and instantly refreshing.  

We ordered the Spring Roll app, Grilled Shrimp with Lemongrass Sauce, and a small Pho Gai (Chicken).  The Spring Roll was cool, crisp and so fresh, with extra large Shrimp seen through the translucent skins.  The Peanut Sauce had a bit of a spicy kick and complemented the sweet crispness of the roll.  The Grilled Shrimp were smaller than in the Roll, but numerous, and arrived nestled in a lettuce leaf.  The Lemongrass Sauce was an interesting taste, less dominant that its Thai sibling, but well used with the shrimp.  It was subtle and I really appreciate the interplay with the light BBQness of the Shrimp. Finally, the soup was Pho-n-tastic; hot broth with large slivers of Chicken Breast and Noodles. The flavor was superb.  This is a clear broth as opposed to the sometimes tawny opaque smoky flavor offered by other pho joints.  Of course, it came with a side plate loaded with sprouts, basil leaves, jalapeno slices and wedge of lime. The bowl was so full when I finished preparing it I had to press down gently with my spoon to suppress the garnish and allow some soup into my spoon. A slightly bigger bowl with the same amount of ingredients might have been a good idea to avoid spillage. Totally satisfying.

We finished with VN Coffee and Green Tea, so we could sit for a while longer and talk while we continued to enjoy the evening created by House of Pho.

This establishment has become first class.  It need offer no apology to sister shops in nearby urban centers.  In fact, I recently returned from Garden Grove, California, the Vietnamese capital in the USA, where I sampled a few Vietnamese delights.  None were any better than House of Pho, and some were considerably worse. 

5 Stars to House of Pho.  Great work, Fred and Victor!

OCTOBER 4, 2014 - The best places continue to evolve as they find their groove and have the courage to explore the outer limits.  House of Pho follows this tradition.  Their menu is broader as they are now offering special seafood dishes, no doubt putting a creative edge to a marketing survey to see where to go next. 

 The house remains clean and coolly inviting.  The staff seems to finally feel comfortable with the kitchen genius, and Fred and Victor reign serenely over their domain. Today's Jackfruit Smoothie was so good ($1. off) and my Com Thit Nuong tasty and full of Pork, Noodles and Spouts.  While I went in for the Pho, the alternatives overcame me, and I went with something new for me.  

Still a 5 Star experience.

JANUARY 5, 2015 - Stopped in last night for a Pho dinner and decided to try the Spicy Hot Wings in their Signature Sauce.  They were hot, crisp, peppery, sweet, fat and delectable. A very good complement to the standard menu. 

Keep it up, guys!

sample_texts:
I had no idea when I decided to check out Basically Burgers in Doylestown that I would be entering a dilemma that required me to chose between fidelity to Yelp's creed of honest no-holds-barred reviews, and my understanding of the Golden Rule of treating people as I wish them to treat me. 

My morality tale began as a walked the center of Doylestown Borough on an incredibly beautiful early August afternoon. Temperature 78; Carolina Blue sky; galleon clouds sailing their airy seas.  Perfect day. 

My destination was Basically Burgers for a simple late lunch.  Yelp led me there, and I had every intention of ordering their best offering loaded with fried onions, jalapenos, and pickled sweet peppers. No cheese today to keep some semblance of austerity. Arriving, I noted the tables and chairs were bare bone, and a black and white mural on the wall that I took to be Doylestown. I wondered if Crayolas would be available to color between the lines. Given the high level activity on the street, I was a bit surprised that this joint was quiet and three quarters empty.  Hhhmmm....

The folks in front of me on line were four seniors who could not decide what they wanted. I took the opportunity to study the wall menu and figure out how to put together my slider sandwich.  Too complicated.  When my moment arrived, I was facing a kindly woman who took pity on my apparent ignorance and asked a few simple questions to lead me through the order process. 

I sat at a small deuce near the front windows admiring the ant colony that Doylestown had become.  The old folks were double dating, and sat at a quad front and center in the main window, chatting about their Medicare. I felt an involuntary shiver run down my spine at the prospect of sitting there one day. 

The burger arrived, delivered by the husband owner chef.  He slapped me on the shoulder as he walked back to the kitchen, with a, "..hope it's hot enough for ya;" referring to the chilis mounded on top of the double disks, and making a bed for them to sit on, as well.  I took a photo to memorialize my favorable first impression. 

It is a shame when great expectations are dashed by poor execution.  This was it.  I asked for a medium burger, but received well done. Little juice left to mix with the pile of fixins, making the first bite experience (FBE) disappointingly dry and with no taste aside from the onion and peppers.  I moaned silently.  I took the ketchup table rocket and shot a long stream of thin red sauce all over the sandwich in an attempt to resuscitate it. Marginally successful, I ate the rest grimly. 

The wife, cashier, waitress came by a few times to ask how I liked my food. She was so innocent and earnest. I smiled weakly. When I finished, I wrote the tip you see elsewhere on the website. 

Now comes the dilemma. 

On my way out of the bathroom I walked past the kitchen, where the chef caught my eye and asked again about the heat of the jalapenos.  I had no qualms with the peppers, so I honestly said they were fine, but I have a high tolerance for heat, and could have used more.  He pointed at another burger in front of him in a red plastic basket. "5 Alarm Burger, if you like 'em to roast your mouth." He listed all the hot ingredients and took out a bottle of his secret hot sauce. He would not let me touch it, but his sly smile told me that he was very proud of the nitro inside. I told him I would have to try it next time (Ugh. Conscience pang). 

Then, he asked me if I was still hungry?  Well, I wasn't really, but where was he going with this line of questions.  "Yes. I could eat," as any good boy would say to his favorite BBQing uncle.  Next thing - he offered me the 5 Alarmer gratis because it was a misorder.  

I was caught; hook, line and sinker. He was making a generous offer because he enjoyed our exchange.  Of course, I took it with a big smile and sat down to devour another double.  This one was actually pretty good.  Much tastier with all the chili varieties piled on, and the secret sauce torching my tongue. Still, too well done for me, but mine was not to complain in the face of unexpected acts of brotherhood and random kindness. 

So, do I write the review I intended to draft after the first burger, or after the second?  Do I allow this real story to influence what I say about this eating experience?  Or, do I just lay it all out for you, and let you draw your own conclusions, and perhaps use it in your sermon next Sunday?

Approaching Rozmaryn (Rosemary) Restaurant on a rainy January night takes a bit of courage.  The store front is a rowhouse with a neon "Open" sign in the window and an old unlit sign beneath it.  The front door is open and all you see is a long hallway with dim yellow lighting.  Sketchy characters on the sidewalk make you involuntarily prepare your fight/flight responses.  Be ready for anything...

But, first walk across Olden Avenue to the liquor store to pick up authentic Polish beer to make the experience complete.  In the back of the store is the Polish section.  More sketchy guys standing around - these 'packin' employees watch your every move.  But, the Xywiec Beer is back there in bottles or cans. I pulled out a six pack of glass and brought it to the counter. $10. minimum for credit cards, so I had to pull out my wallet (I really didn't want to do it).  Done. Walked back across the street and up the front steps of 925.  On the first door to the left was the restaurant sign bidding we enter.  What was behind the door? 

I went in first.  I was greeted by an apartment converted into a small eatery.  Yellow walls, stained wood trim. Wall sconces, but little other adornment.  No one else in the main dining area.  We stood a moment and waited to see if someone would come out to seat us.  A few long seconds later a woman appeared from the rear with a warm smile and firm voice welcoming us to her shop.  She was the owner, hostess, and chef.  Very big persona.  Lovely, and I immediately relaxed.  At 6:30 we expected other patrons, but we were the first and only folks there all night.  She sat us at a central table for 4, allowing us to spread out.  NFL Playoffs had depleted her clientele, but we were quite happy to have all her attention.  

After getting the lay of the land from the gracious Madame, we ordered off the rather small menu, which is in both English and Polish.  The Polish Plate to share as our appetizer.  It consisted of Meat Pierogies, Potato Pancakes, Kielbasa in Yellow Mustard, and Apple Sauce.  The Pierogies were the least enjoyable part of the dish. While the dough was lightly fried and was a good consistency, the meat was too dense and organ-tasting for me. The Potato Pancakes were large and thin, not very oily, and a good mix of potato and flour (to hold them together).  They were crispy on the edges and tasty with a dollop of Apple Sauce to mix a little sweetness with the mild saltiness of the latke. The Kielbasa was excellent.  Scored before grilling to help the heat penetrate and to avoid splitting, the outer skin was just firm enough to hold the link together, but when bitten, it gave way nicely to allow the very tender innards to meet the tongue, disclosing its softness and interesting spice.  It was the surprise star of the plate.  The mustard kicked up the flavor, but did not overwhelm the essence of the sausage.  

We had the Stuffed Cabbage and Breaded Chicken Cutlet as our entries.  The Cabbage dish came with Mashed Potato and Cucumber Salad.  The Cabbage was tender and the mix of beef and something else was good, but perhaps a little bland.  It needed something to sweeten it, or at least that was how my Polish-Jewish grandmother prepared them. The potatoes were not hot enough, and seemed to have been plated before visiting the oven. Mild disappointment.  The Cucumber Salad was a cream base and decent.  

My Chicken dish was excellent.  The breast was large, with a pleasantly hand dipped breading coating it.  I opted for Mushrooms rather than Tomato Sauce, and was very pleased with the freshness and amount piled on the chicken.  Very tasty and definitely something I would order again.  The mashed were again cool and I left most of them alone.  The Red Beet Salad finished the plate.  Served appropriately cold, it was a nice fit, bringing mild sweetness to the mix of flavors in the breading, chicken, and shrooms.  I was happy.

To complete the repast, we ordered the Blintzes stuffed with Farmer's Cheese, and dusted with Powdered Sugar.  These babies were wonderful.  Perfect crepe wrap, and a whipped sweet cheesy filling.  Light, and a perfect end to the meal with freshly brewed coffee.  

This is a one of a kind richly local ethnic urban place that is easy to love.  The entire experience from the sidewalk scene, to the liquor store, to the special charm of the owner/chef, make Rozmaryn worth a visit or two.  When a meal becomes the food for a short story you have found something remarkable. 

This was definitely that, and more.

My first time to Indian Garden, a destination hidden in the shopping center on Stony Hill Road in Lower Makefield (we all know it is not really Yardley).  It is catercorner from the Giant Supermarket, and next door to Vesuvio Pizza. 
Walking in the first impression is that the space is darkly lit, and in combination with the aroma of curry, cardamon, and other spices, feels a little like entering a bazaar on the subcontinent.  As the eyes adjust, white table clothes appear on small square and large round tables reflecting the dim recessed lighting and the illumination over the buffet tables. They are a little close together, requiring a bob and weave to access the back tables. Still, very engaging way to start the experience.  The staff in black and white with ties is super efficient and helpful. 
I went with the buffet to be able to sample more than would be possible ordering off the menu, although another reviewer warned off doing this.  I tried : Vegetable Pakora (not hot enough, and thus a little oily), Chicken Tikka Masala (very good), Chicken Kadai (good and smokey flavored), Chicken Makhani (nice creamy tomato/ginger sauce), Vegetable Korma (was that frozen veggies in a good Korma?), Chana Saag (very enjoyable), Aloo Bhartha (love eggplant in all incarnations), Paneer Makhani (the cheese reminds of tofu, but decent), Naan (quite good, but wanted garlic), Mango Lassi (excellent), and mango pudding (really?). Ok. I won't have dinner tonight. 
Total Cost: $12.50
Given the almost total absence of Indian food in the immediate area, Indian Garden is very good, and worth another trip.  Next time, I will order off the menu and see what shakes out.  Upgrades are definitely possible once the larger menu is penetrated and surrenders its secrets.

9.28.13 - Returned for another lunch buffet today with a few campaign people. The floor was not crowded when we arrived, but slowly filled after 1:00.  I think I was placed at the same table as my first visit. I love when that happens. Kismet. The food was as good as last time, and the Naan changed from Regular to Onion while we were there, and added to the sop up enjoyment of all the rich sauces mixing on the plate.  I was a bit distracted with conversation so did not commit to memory what I ate, but I thoroughly enjoyed the mix of flavors, textures, and basics. 
3 Stars with a Bullet.  This place is going to move up soon.

11.11.16 UPDATE - Sometimes the best intentions to return to a restaurant soon to followup on good early impressions are swallowed by life taking you elsewhere. So it was with Indian Garden. Job changes, new food outlets, or just plain forgetfulness, play a hand. Still, I finally came back here last Friday night with family when we decided we hungered for local spice. 

We were seated promptly without reservations at 6 by the smiling hostess. The large dining room was half empty on arrival, but filled quickly with some recognizable faces. Just beat the rush. 

The three of us ordered Veggie Samosa and Chili Pakora apps. Both good, but the Pakora had a too thick fried coating over the pepper and filling. Reminded of a Relleno, but not as smoothly integrated. The Chicken Curry was fresh with a nice red mild heat. Our Lamb Korma had grilled baa-baa pieces in a smooth and almond sweet beige sauce, that was the star of the show. The third dish was a potato and eggplant combo with chunkiness and light spice finish without a pond around it. Their Basket of Naan completed our table. 

Everything was solid, if not exceptional, with a tendency to underspice all their dishes, perhaps in tune with the palates of their clientele. Not a criticism, but an adjustment to reality. Yet, if a stronger  South Asian taste is what you seek, just ask for max heat, or try the local alternatives (Guru, Dream Spicy), where there is less accommodation to neighborhood preference for dilution. 

Still, the quality of the meal was undeniable. A little fine tuning will achieve the heat quotient desired. Indian Garden is fan worthy.

gap_reviews.text:
The owners of Pin Wei Chinese Restaurant on S. Henderson Road, King of Prussia, PA, may be Taiwanese, but they have taken the time to understand and produce first quality Szechuanese cuisine in the heart of Montgomery County. They have mastered the mysteries of extremely spicy sauces and cooking techniques that create the unique numbing style that finds its source in the history of Chengdu and Chongqing, China.  There aren't many of these authentic houses around, and when one is found it automatically shoots to the top of the list of great dinner spots.  From the size of the crowd waiting for tables last night, the secret is out. 

This was our first visit, after looking mostly in vain for a Szechuan chili joint that knew what it was doing.  There are a few in Philly, and a couple in nearby Jersey, but by and large, the art of flamethrower dishes is a lost art.  We were very happily impressed with Pin Wei. 

We ordered more than we could finish in one sitting, so we would have enough to take home and enjoy a second time.  Our choices consisted of: Scallion Oil Cucumber as an appetizer, and entrees of Chicken Dry Chili Peppers, Lamb Dry Pot, Twice Cooked Fish, and Deep Fried Longhots. 

In a word; FIRE! 

The cucumbers were served cold and marinating in the clear scallion oil.  Crisp, cool, mildly hot and a great introduction.  The platters arrived as they were finished in the kitchen, not all at once, in the traditional Chinese custom.  The longhots arrived next.  They were beautiful to behold - like dark green snakes coiled on themselves waiting for someone to bite them to release their venom.  Awesome, as each chew released more toxin, burning the gums and walls of the mouth, and bringing tears to the eyes.  Eventually, the scald tempered, and the underlying flavor of these peppers emerged transcendent. 

The chicken dry pepper was a sight to behold, with a huge amount of small red Szechuan chilies, garlic, ginger, onion and small pieces of breaded and twice deep fried chicken mixed throughout.  The chili powder on the morsels was a powerful, sinus clearing, blast.  With every wonderful crunch more capsaicin was released, bringing the mouth to near full numbness.  Got to love that feeling to appreciate its otherworldliness. The lamb dry pot arrived next, and we were thankful for a little relief from the conflagration.  The meat was tender with a gentle sweetness beneath the cumin and other stimulants.  Plenty of mushrooms to absorb the dark sauce at the bottom of the heated metal bowl.  Excellent.

Finally, the twice cooked fish arrived. There was a small glitch when after waiting a while, the wrong dish came to the table - twice cooked pork (belly), which looked good, but, alas, was not what we ordered. Ten more minutes to get the right platter.  It was terrific, saving best for last.  Fish fillets, slightly crisp, yet also soft within, with a dark sauce that hinted of vinegar in addition to the less dominant burning powders.  I had to ask our server what that unusual taste was, but he just smiled and demurred.

Inscrutable. 

The secrets of Pin Wei were safe with him. After all, he was the owner, and he didn't want us trying to replicate his magic elixirs at home. He didn't want to be responsible for what might happen.

HOT Damn, people! 

PRO TIP - How does Pin Wei compare with Han Dynasty in Old City?  Food wise, it is a very close call.  Both are excellent, and there is no point in picking one over the other based on the kitchen.  Where there is some separation is in the atmospherics.  Pin Wei is a small place compared to Chestnut Street.  The dining room is very crowded and thus quite loud.  It is a bit distracting. HanDynasty is in an old bank building, and although busy, possesses more nooks and crannies where it is possible to feel some level of  privacy, even in the crowd. The front of Pin Wei was packed with people waiting for tables, and we almost had to fight our way out after dinner. The bar area was SRO, while Han has a larger bar zone, which seldom feels overwhelmed with people.

It is obvious than the success of Pin Wei, is also its shortcoming.  It needs a much bigger space.  Whether it wants it or not is a separate question.

Legend tells of a mythologic Blue Moose that traveled the spiritland and Doan hills around New Hope and the north river highlands. From time immemorial it was the guardian that helped those hurt by natural, supernatural and even manmade acts, survive and repair their damaged bodies and souls.  Its gifts to all were amazing strength, nurturing support and a supply of local foodstuff to aid in recovery. It was also known for dexterity on the mandolin.  It seldom took sides. It was only there to observe and assist with sustenance and music.  

Over the centuries Blue Moose has taken various forms animate and inanimate. An accomplished shape shifter, it appeared when the need was dire in a form acceptable in the current cultural milieu. It helped sustain our men during the Revolution, and helped build the longboats that made the Christmas eve crossing. Currently, it assumed the guise of a restaurant in a small old house on Mechanic Street in New Hope, Pa. Always brash, it has not bothered to hide its identity. 

It sits in plain sight.  Café Blue Moose welcomes all to rest within its aging walls and taste its well honed victuals at any of its tables. 

It was within these atmospherics that we arrived for an early dinner last Friday evening, climbed the stairs to the second floor and took control of the middle table overlooking the open space down to the front room.  Open paper umbrellas hung down gently spinning in the air currents.  Stars were painted on the exposed cathedral ceiling.  Period pieces on the walls completed the sense that we were encouraged to take leave of normalcy and enter the world of whimsy, where we could best appreciate the decompression from reality.  BYOB aided the transformation. 

Our servers were lighthearted and sincere.  The menu a prix fixe affair allowing choices in any combination of Apps/Salad/Soups, Entrees, and Desserts that added up to 2 or 3.  Our choices were to share a Warm Goat Cheese Salad; Braised Short Ribs and Sautéed Salmon; and a Apple Crisp finale. The salad was a nice green plate with local leaves, Hazelnuts, sliced tart Green Apple and Dried Cherries in Balsamic Vinaigrette .  A round fried Goat Cheese ball was indeed hot, smooth, and subtle.  The parts played very well together and was a delight.  

The Short Rib was already off bone having bathed a few hours in red wine and garlic.  The meat was sitting atop Garlic Mashed, and under a strong helping of Pickled Cabbage and Carrots.  The taste was mildly sweetly vinegar inspired, and completely pliant.  The Mashed and Rib sauces were blood brothers, bringing the best of both when combined.  Symbiotic to the max.

The Salmon was a reasonably sized steak on top of small grain White Rice Risotto.  It, too, had a pickled topside that tasted of onion/mango, but was likely something else.  It was finished well and easy to eat, although there with an unidentifiable aftertaste that was less enjoyable than the primary sensation.  A small nit in an otherwise inspired meal.  

Finally, the Warm Apple Crisp was a fun crunchy a la mode mess with an oat/brown sugar crumble that was a joyful indulgence.  

All the while, Blue Moose played his music in the background, completing a near-epic experience.  His mandolin put aside for a heavy bass line for rock melodies that soothed and awakened appreciation of meal and history.  

As we stepped out into the snowfall and gathered our memories of what had just transpired, the sense that Big Blue was with us in deed and spirit was palpable.  We thanked him for his faithfulness and skilled metamorphosis into a shelter that was exactly what was needed in this age of doubt and cynicism.

sample_texts:
"It was the best of times. It was the worst of times."  - Charles Dickens, Tale of Two Cities. 

With apologies to the master, my experience at Empire Szechuan resembles that opening line in the classic novel; in reverse.

We were just looking for a decent meal after excellent foot massage down Bustleton at Lavender Foot Spa.  I saw the sign and was curious without checking Yelp first.  Spontaneity can lead to wonderful discovery, or just bust your balls.  This was a strange hybrid of both. 

Walking in, we quickly realized this was primarily a take out joint.  Two tables out front, with one occupied by the owner's sons playing computer games.  It was also chilly inside with everyone wearing jackets to keep the electric bill down.  I love Szechuanese cuisine and hoped the name on the sign reflected the product in the kitchen, when so many others simply do not.  

We ordered at the counter. Apple ordering and pay system.  Reading the menu, it was obvious there was precious little Chengdu kitchen here.  Still, we agreed to ride it out and see what happened.  Orange Beef, Eggplant in Garlic Sauce, and Steamed Dumplings, were our desire.  We sat at the sole open table to wait, and hoped for the hot tea to warm us.  

It arrived in a quart sized plastic soup container, with tea bags inside.  No teapot. No ceramic teacups.  Just styro cups. Free pour by bending the rim into a spout. Really?!  Before we could pour the tea,  our place settings were brought out.  Styro plates and skimpy plastic forks, with those take out packets of duck sauce, soy and mustard thrown on top.  

Feeling so appreciated right there!  

We poured the cha and waited for the next act to unfold.  This was either going to be a disaster, or outright conflagration, if their slovenliness continued.  We scored a pair of chopsticks, which were much sturdier than the malformed forks.

Then the moon broke through the brooding clouds.  

The Eggplant dish arrived first.  It was a large amount of the veggie, with other colorful edibles mixed in.  This was way more than could fit in a Chinese take out box, and it glistened from the oily sauce that held it together.  We opened the rice box to shovel some on the plates as our base for eating, and sticked a few pieces of the purple nightshade on top.  We were guarded lest the load prove inedible.  

We looked at each other in astonishment as the taste registered in our pleasure centered brains.  It was excellent!  Soft eggplant.  Appropriately cooked secondaries. Wonderful garlic sauce that was just prepared for our meal.  Yes. It was rather incongruous after the inauspicious start to the dinner, but one we gladly welcomed.  

The Orange Beef arrived moments later.  Another huge pile of food, this time, dark brown spicy gloop on top of a motherload of nicely sliced beef, that had been wokked and rolled in a light batter and deep fried before returning to the stove for the final pouring of the Tso-like sauce.  

Munch. Chew. Blooming, mouth expanding flavor of orange and chili, that had my eyes open wide in silent exclamation.  What the hey?!  This was even better than the eggplant.  The meat seemed fresh; not frozen, and again the sauce was prepared just for us.  It tasted as good as almost any orange beef I had ever eaten.  We agreed that we were enjoying an exceptional Chinese American meal, especially given the dive we were in, and the way the story began.

Quite frankly, we were blown away.

Maybe the secret of this place is to overlook the distain of the sons, and the stain of the environment, and just hunker down in the winter heatlessness of the house, knowing something those who only do take out and delivery never discover - that the food at Empire Szechuan is a slam bang winner for those who sit there and wait to eat right out of the wok. By the time it arrives to your home, the magic has disappeared.  Astounding! 

We packed up the leftovers in anticipation of a second round of slammin' Chinois, suspecting it would not be anything like what we had just devoured.  We paid the twenty bucks or so, and walked out into the night slightly inebriated from this rags to riches saga.

PS. The dumplings sucked (not XLB), but we forgave them after this profane/sacred epiphany.

The owners of Pin Wei Chinese Restaurant on S. Henderson Road, King of Prussia, PA, may be Taiwanese, but they have taken the time to understand and produce first quality Szechuanese cuisine in the heart of Montgomery County. They have mastered the mysteries of extremely spicy sauces and cooking techniques that create the unique numbing style that finds its source in the history of Chengdu and Chongqing, China.  There aren't many of these authentic houses around, and when one is found it automatically shoots to the top of the list of great dinner spots.  From the size of the crowd waiting for tables last night, the secret is out. 

This was our first visit, after looking mostly in vain for a Szechuan chili joint that knew what it was doing.  There are a few in Philly, and a couple in nearby Jersey, but by and large, the art of flamethrower dishes is a lost art.  We were very happily impressed with Pin Wei. 

We ordered more than we could finish in one sitting, so we would have enough to take home and enjoy a second time.  Our choices consisted of: Scallion Oil Cucumber as an appetizer, and entrees of Chicken Dry Chili Peppers, Lamb Dry Pot, Twice Cooked Fish, and Deep Fried Longhots. 

In a word; FIRE! 

The cucumbers were served cold and marinating in the clear scallion oil.  Crisp, cool, mildly hot and a great introduction.  The platters arrived as they were finished in the kitchen, not all at once, in the traditional Chinese custom.  The longhots arrived next.  They were beautiful to behold - like dark green snakes coiled on themselves waiting for someone to bite them to release their venom.  Awesome, as each chew released more toxin, burning the gums and walls of the mouth, and bringing tears to the eyes.  Eventually, the scald tempered, and the underlying flavor of these peppers emerged transcendent. 

The chicken dry pepper was a sight to behold, with a huge amount of small red Szechuan chilies, garlic, ginger, onion and small pieces of breaded and twice deep fried chicken mixed throughout.  The chili powder on the morsels was a powerful, sinus clearing, blast.  With every wonderful crunch more capsaicin was released, bringing the mouth to near full numbness.  Got to love that feeling to appreciate its otherworldliness. The lamb dry pot arrived next, and we were thankful for a little relief from the conflagration.  The meat was tender with a gentle sweetness beneath the cumin and other stimulants.  Plenty of mushrooms to absorb the dark sauce at the bottom of the heated metal bowl.  Excellent.

Finally, the twice cooked fish arrived. There was a small glitch when after waiting a while, the wrong dish came to the table - twice cooked pork (belly), which looked good, but, alas, was not what we ordered. Ten more minutes to get the right platter.  It was terrific, saving best for last.  Fish fillets, slightly crisp, yet also soft within, with a dark sauce that hinted of vinegar in addition to the less dominant burning powders.  I had to ask our server what that unusual taste was, but he just smiled and demurred.

Inscrutable. 

The secrets of Pin Wei were safe with him. After all, he was the owner, and he didn't want us trying to replicate his magic elixirs at home. He didn't want to be responsible for what might happen.

HOT Damn, people! 

PRO TIP - How does Pin Wei compare with Han Dynasty in Old City?  Food wise, it is a very close call.  Both are excellent, and there is no point in picking one over the other based on the kitchen.  Where there is some separation is in the atmospherics.  Pin Wei is a small place compared to Chestnut Street.  The dining room is very crowded and thus quite loud.  It is a bit distracting. HanDynasty is in an old bank building, and although busy, possesses more nooks and crannies where it is possible to feel some level of  privacy, even in the crowd. The front of Pin Wei was packed with people waiting for tables, and we almost had to fight our way out after dinner. The bar area was SRO, while Han has a larger bar zone, which seldom feels overwhelmed with people.

It is obvious than the success of Pin Wei, is also its shortcoming.  It needs a much bigger space.  Whether it wants it or not is a separate question.

Legend tells of a mythologic Blue Moose that traveled the spiritland and Doan hills around New Hope and the north river highlands. From time immemorial it was the guardian that helped those hurt by natural, supernatural and even manmade acts, survive and repair their damaged bodies and souls.  Its gifts to all were amazing strength, nurturing support and a supply of local foodstuff to aid in recovery. It was also known for dexterity on the mandolin.  It seldom took sides. It was only there to observe and assist with sustenance and music.  

Over the centuries Blue Moose has taken various forms animate and inanimate. An accomplished shape shifter, it appeared when the need was dire in a form acceptable in the current cultural milieu. It helped sustain our men during the Revolution, and helped build the longboats that made the Christmas eve crossing. Currently, it assumed the guise of a restaurant in a small old house on Mechanic Street in New Hope, Pa. Always brash, it has not bothered to hide its identity. 

It sits in plain sight.  Café Blue Moose welcomes all to rest within its aging walls and taste its well honed victuals at any of its tables. 

It was within these atmospherics that we arrived for an early dinner last Friday evening, climbed the stairs to the second floor and took control of the middle table overlooking the open space down to the front room.  Open paper umbrellas hung down gently spinning in the air currents.  Stars were painted on the exposed cathedral ceiling.  Period pieces on the walls completed the sense that we were encouraged to take leave of normalcy and enter the world of whimsy, where we could best appreciate the decompression from reality.  BYOB aided the transformation. 

Our servers were lighthearted and sincere.  The menu a prix fixe affair allowing choices in any combination of Apps/Salad/Soups, Entrees, and Desserts that added up to 2 or 3.  Our choices were to share a Warm Goat Cheese Salad; Braised Short Ribs and Sautéed Salmon; and a Apple Crisp finale. The salad was a nice green plate with local leaves, Hazelnuts, sliced tart Green Apple and Dried Cherries in Balsamic Vinaigrette .  A round fried Goat Cheese ball was indeed hot, smooth, and subtle.  The parts played very well together and was a delight.  

The Short Rib was already off bone having bathed a few hours in red wine and garlic.  The meat was sitting atop Garlic Mashed, and under a strong helping of Pickled Cabbage and Carrots.  The taste was mildly sweetly vinegar inspired, and completely pliant.  The Mashed and Rib sauces were blood brothers, bringing the best of both when combined.  Symbiotic to the max.

The Salmon was a reasonably sized steak on top of small grain White Rice Risotto.  It, too, had a pickled topside that tasted of onion/mango, but was likely something else.  It was finished well and easy to eat, although there with an unidentifiable aftertaste that was less enjoyable than the primary sensation.  A small nit in an otherwise inspired meal.  

Finally, the Warm Apple Crisp was a fun crunchy a la mode mess with an oat/brown sugar crumble that was a joyful indulgence.  

All the while, Blue Moose played his music in the background, completing a near-epic experience.  His mandolin put aside for a heavy bass line for rock melodies that soothed and awakened appreciation of meal and history.  

As we stepped out into the snowfall and gathered our memories of what had just transpired, the sense that Big Blue was with us in deed and spirit was palpable.  We thanked him for his faithfulness and skilled metamorphosis into a shelter that was exactly what was needed in this age of doubt and cynicism.

gap_reviews.text:
East Kensington Avenue is about the grittiest urban zone in Philadelphia. With early 20th Century elevated subway (the El) rumbling above the shadowed street below, it can be difficult to see any hope of renewal. Yet, a closer look shows seeds germinating in the cracks in the sidewalk. 

The 3900 block has several Southeast Asian restaurants that are more than just surviving, but thriving in this unforgiving environment. Vietnamese, Lao, Chinese and Thai kitchens are turning out some of the best cuisine in the city. I drove down last weekend to explore some of the possibilities, and selected Vientiane Bistro as my starting point. 

Right next door to the well known Cafe Pho Ga Thanh Thanh, the Bistro is nicely renovated with utilitarian metal furniture, plank floors and simple wall decorations. The aroma of lemongrass, ginger and garlic greeted me at the door, quickly followed by the attractive, friendly server pointing me to a deuce in front along the side wall. She returned with menu a moment later. She was unaware that there was also a lunch list and when I inquired, she confirmed with the owner that it was available as well. She retrieved one promptly. My Yelp research paid off, giving me a chance to sample a few smaller portions of more dishes; Lao style dim sum .

Coconut Soup, Veggie Spring Roll, Chicken Pad Kee Mao (Drunken Noodles) and Tamarind Wings appetizer, were soon being prepared by their talented chef. I was about to be entertained by a four act play. 

The soup was mildly sweet, full of veggies and a nice place to start. Loved every slurp and picked up the bowl to drain every drop. The six Tamarind Wings were large, grilled and bathed in a dark rouge/amber thick tart glaze. After the soup, it presented a very different taste and texture. A lover of tamarind duck, this was a great counterpoint and subtle reminder of that favorite. The Spring Roll was the weak link, but with the sweet dipping sauce was more of a bridge to what came next. I asked for the Pad Kee Mao to be done "extra spicy." That was what I got. Each bite of the veggies, chicken and broad noodles ignited my lips, gums and tongue with a pleasant burn, that gradually increased as I worked my way down the large mound in the wide bowl. Never numbing, I felt as much as tasted every morsel. Special surprise at the bottom of the bowl, where all the oils and spices collected and covered the last few pieces of food. I knew I probably shouldn't, but I couldn't resist spooning all of that into my mouth. 

Holy cow! It was a hot blast that brought tears to my eyes and blew out my sinuses. Tears of joy, I assure you. Should have asked for more napkins, but it all ended well. 

The influence of Lao and Thai cuisine on each other is wonderful. The hybrid creates a flavor/texture profile that connects the delicacy of Thai sauces with the heavier heartiness of Lao cooking. That is a beautifully delicious thing and a place that I could live happily forever.

Cryo Spa Natural Healing Center in the Holland Shopping Center, Holland, PA, is a holistic alternative health services center at the intersection of Holland and Buck Roads. It can be a little challenging to reach if coming down Buck Road, but with patience the traffic and red lights can be overcome.  It is well worth your effort to find your way there. 

Although cryotherapy (exposing your body to extremely cold temperature for a short period of time to deal with autoimmune conditions through the release of endorphins and anti-inflammatory proteins) is the main attraction, I was there on a first visit for a full body massage to address lingering lower back issues. I was greeted promptly by the front desk person, who asked me to complete three pages of information regarding my medical history and reason for coming. Before I was finished, my masseuse Marla was introducing herself with warm smile and firm handshake.
 
Before starting the massage, I was also introduced to Lucy, the owner of the facility.  She offered to give me a quick tour.  She showed me the cryotherapy equipment, shiatsu and mediation rooms, and finally the massage chamber where Marla would attempt to loosen my lumbar/sacral spine and take my discomfort away.  The place is bigger than it looks from the outside, and Lucy was very knowledgeable about all the modalities offered. 
 
When it was time to start my massage, Marla directed me to disrobe and get under the sheets on the table, face down on the headrest. She left the room while I did so. Upon returning, she asked a few questions about the strength of the massage desired (strong), and instructed me to tell her if I was experiencing pain (Don't worry. I don't deal with pain in silence.) She explained that she would be doing a combination of stretches, deep tissue, pressure point and Swedish techniques which she labeled as, "Sports Massage."  

She was amazing.  Her hands intuitively found the discomfort in my back and legs, and dug, kneaded, lifted/stretched my joints, and actually moved the muscles, tendons and ligaments around to release their tension and help them find their proper place in my body alignment. She had incredible strength, and was able to lift each leg, pull it further upward, while manipulating the musculature and joints. All the while, we conversed quietly about how I was feeling, giving me directions to help her, and informing me about her experience in massage and healthcare.  Fascinating woman.  Excellent healer. 

After my hour, I arose slowly.  I felt looser and longer.  More significantly, a sense of wellbeing and mild euphoria surrounded me. I was extremely relaxed and felt a limberness and flexibility that had been absent for a long while. I slept very well last night. 

This morning, even as I write this review, my body feels like it is glowing. I know I will return to CryoSpa regularly to maintain and improve this sensation of health, strength and vitality. 

Marla did great work. I also want to give the Shiatsu a try, and if brave enough, go into the cryospa itself to see what that can do. 

Totally positive first impression.

Please don't get me wrong. I love DeLorenzo's tomato pies, and have been amazed by them since moving to Trenton many years ago and discovering the Italian wonders of the Chambersburg neighborhood. I, like most of those fabulous Roman kitchens, have since left the city for greener lawns in the suburbs, but still returned to Hudson Street, and Robbinsville thereafter, just to continue our love affair with their thin crust tomato pies, that were the best I had ever eaten until that time.

They have since expanded into Bucks County, with their newest venture on Edgewood Road in Lower Makefield, PA. (No, it is not Yardley).  I couldn't wait to have the opportunity to visit their new digs. That finally happened yesterday on a Sunday evening. I had ordered by phone a few hours before to be certain they could handle my request given how busy their joint normally was on weekends. I was assured that my two large pies (one regular and one sausage) could be handled by the designated pick up time of 7:00 PM.  

When I arrived, the smallish parking lot was overflowing, but I lucked into someone pulling out just as I pulled in.  Their store is all new construction alongside the Giant Shopping Center off Stony Hill Road. As expected, the waiting area was jammed with people waiting for tables.  I approached the very busy cashier, and when he had a second to look at me, gave him my name for the takeout order. He checked in the kitchen and relayed that my pies were not yet out of the oven.

Perfect! I would secure them at apex heat and transport them home while still baking inside.  I reflexively began to salivate at the thought of biting into the steaming chunky red gravy, mozzarella and thin cracker crust. Oh, Yeah!

The ten minute wait was fine with me. I used the time to take photos and watch all the happy customers. The aroma was timeless, and threw me into reveries of my earliest experience with DeLo's grand pies. I eventually paid the $35 for the two artisanal works of edible art, and drove home with visions of plum tomatoes and basil dancing in my head. 

The sausage pie was just as amazing as I remembered. Local crumbled meat, fresh melted mozza, and mildly salty thin crust to complement the equally mildly sweet red sauce. The explosion in my mouth was a wonderful party with old friends. Unfortunately, the regular pie was overbaked, with too much crust burn and too little sauce filling the irregularly shaped base. I like some scorching as much as anyone, but this one was beyond that - complete carbon in too many places. This can happen when the kitchen staff is stretched to the max and doesn't watch each pie through the process. Chalk it up to a bad card in an otherwise sound hand. 

DeLorenzo's is still about the best tomato pie in the area, especially on this side of the Delaware River.  Very glad to save the longish drive to Robbinsville, NJ,  although their cousin's shop in Levittown is just as good.

drifts: Stopped emphasizing: where, after, before, place
Started emphasizing: kitchen, better, around, enough

narrative:
He’s a moralist with a soft spot for redemption arcs, the kind of guy who treats every meal like a potential parable. Early on, he agonized over ethics like a dinner-table philosopher, torn between Yelp’s brutal honesty and the human warmth of a chef sliding him a free burger. Back then, he noticed thresholds—*where* he entered, *before* the first bite, *after* the check came—because he was still mapping the terrain of experience. Now, he’s less interested in entrances than in kitchens: the engine room where integrity is tested. His language has shifted from spatial to qualitative—*better, enough, around*—not because he’s pickier, but because he’s learned that consistency and competence matter more than charm. He forgives dry chicken if the kitchen shows heart, but won’t abide a lack of self-awareness. He’s drawn to places that don’t pretend—Lao bistro in a scarred Philly block, a cryo spa in a strip mall—because he respects resilience over polish. He still writes like he’s drafting sermons, but they’re less about judgment and more about witness. The food is never just food; it’s evidence of character.  

He would never forgive a place that mistakes indifference for authenticity.
